memphite.creator: Ptah
memphite.theology: Ptah creates through his heart and tongue

name: From Nun to the Ennead
description:
Egyptian creation mythology is not a single narrative but multiple accounts from different theological centers,
                each emphasizing different aspects of how order emerged from chaos. These myths share common themes: the
                primordial waters, self-creation, divine speech/thought, and the establishment of Ma'at (cosmic order).

keyMyths.title: The Emergence from Nun
keyMyths.description: In the beginning, there was only Nun, the primordial waters of chaos. From this watery abyss, Atum, the self-created deity, arose, marking the initial separation of order from disorder and the beginning of creation.

keyMyths.title: Atum's Self-Creation
keyMyths.description: Atum, existing alone in the void, created Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture) through masturbation or sneezing (accounts vary). This act of self-procreation marked the first act of differentiation within the undifferentiated Nun.

keyMyths.title: The Separation of Geb and Nut
keyMyths.description: Shu, the air, separated Geb (earth) and Nut (sky), who were locked in an embrace, creating space for life to exist. This separation allowed for the passage of the sun and the establishment of a habitable world.

keyMyths.title: The Birth of the First Gods
keyMyths.description: From Shu and Tefnut came Geb and Nut, who in turn gave birth to Osiris, Isis, Seth, and Nephthys. These deities represent fundamental aspects of the world and the divine order, forming the core of the Egyptian pantheon.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Humanity
keyMyths.description: Humanity was created either from the tears of Ra or from clay by Khnum, depending on the myth. This act populated the world and established the relationship between gods and mortals, with humans responsible for maintaining Ma'at.

sources.name: The Pyramid Texts
sources.description: The oldest collection of Egyptian religious texts, containing spells and hymns intended to ensure the pharaoh's successful afterlife. Offers insight into early cosmological beliefs.
sources.title: The Pyramid Texts

sources.name: The Book of the Dead
sources.description: A collection of spells and prayers intended to guide the deceased through the underworld. Includes versions of creation myths and cosmological concepts.
sources.title: The Book of the Dead

sources.name: The Coffin Texts
sources.description: A collection of spells and funerary texts inscribed on coffins during the Middle Kingdom. Provides further elaboration on cosmological themes and the afterlife.
sources.title: The Coffin Texts

sources.name: The Memphite Theology
sources.description: A text inscribed on the Shabaka Stone that presents a Memphite version of the creation myth, emphasizing the role of Ptah in creating the world through thought and speech.
sources.title: The Memphite Theology

sources.name: Karenga, Maulana. *Maat, The Moral Ideal in Ancient Egypt: A Study in Classical African Ethics*.
sources.description: A modern scholarly work analyzing the concept of Ma'at and its importance in ancient Egyptian society and cosmology.
sources.title: Karenga, Maulana. *Maat, The Moral Ideal in Ancient Egypt: A Study in Classical African Ethics*.
